title: Basic loci of Shimura varieties
speaker: Xuhua He
affiliation: University of Maryland; von Neumann Fellow, School of Mathematics
abstract: In mod-$p$ reductions of modular curves, there is a finite set of supersingular points and its open complement corresponding to ordinary elliptic curves. In the study of mod-$p$ reductions of more general Shimura varieties, there is a "Newton stratification" decomposing the reduction into finitely many locally closed subsets, of which exactly one is closed. This closed set is called the basic locus; it recovers the supersingular locus in the classical case of modular curves.

proof_stages.label: Motivating phenomenon and guiding questions
proof_stages.summary: The talk isolates the phenomenon that, in certain Shimura varieties, the basic locus in the special fiber is a union of classical Deligne-Lusztig varieties indexed by Bruhat-Tits-building data, and formulates the questions of when this occurs, why it occurs, and what additional geometric consequences follow.

proof_stages.label: Group-theoretic setup for special fibers
proof_stages.summary: Assuming good integral models and five axioms relating the special fiber to the local group, the speaker defines the maps to parahoric double cosets and to B(G), identifies their natural ranges as Adm(μ) and B(G,μ), and interprets their fibers as Kottwitz-Rapoport/EKOR and Newton strata, with the minimal Newton class giving the basic locus.

proof_stages.label: Equivalence theorem and classification
proof_stages.summary: Under the axioms, four conditions are stated as equivalent: a classical-Deligne-Lusztig union description of the basic locus, a central-leaf description of every nonbasic Newton stratum, Hodge-Newton decomposability for every nonbasic class in B(G,μ), and an explicit root-theoretic inequality on μ; this yields a case-by-case classification including unitary and orthogonal families and a few exceptional cases such as SP4 and U(2,2).

proof_stages.label: Hodge-Newton decomposability mechanism
proof_stages.summary: Hodge-Newton decomposability is explained via a common breakpoint of the Newton polygon and the μ-polygon, and the usual decomposition into a Levi Rapoport-Zink or affine Deligne-Lusztig problem is extended from hyperspecial level to arbitrary parahoric level, where several σ-stable parabolic/Levi pieces may appear.

proof_stages.label: Group-theoretic part of the proof
proof_stages.summary: The proof first identifies conditions (3) and (4) by group-theoretic arguments, uses the inequality in (4) to obtain the classification, and notes that the converse implication from a nice basic-locus description back to the inequality requires case-by-case analysis.

proof_stages.label: Geometric implications among the conditions
proof_stages.summary: The remaining proof uses Hodge-Newton decomposition plus the μ-inequality to reduce nonbasic strata to zero-dimensional Harris-Taylor-type Levi pieces, giving condition (2), and then uses EKOR stratification together with classical Deligne-Lusztig geometry to make the basic locus a union of classical Deligne-Lusztig varieties, giving condition (1).

examples.name: Ramified GU(1,2) and GU(1,3) basic loci
examples.note: The comparison shows the desired 'nice' basic-locus picture concretely: components are projective lines with four special points, but the number of components through a special point differs, illustrating the Bruhat-Tits-building incidence data.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker draws/describes the GU(1,2) and GU(1,3) basic loci, identifying P1 components, special points, and the different intersection counts

examples.name: Siegel SP6 as a failure of the classical-Deligne-Lusztig picture
examples.note: The Siegel case beyond SP4 is used as a counterpoint showing that the theorem is genuinely restrictive and that affine Deligne-Lusztig geometry usually cannot be replaced by classical Deligne-Lusztig varieties.
examples.engagement_reason: SP6 is cited as the point where the classical Deligne-Lusztig description fails, but no concrete computation or construction is done for it

examples.name: SP4 admissible set in the affine Weyl group
examples.note: In response to a question about the size of the admissible set, the speaker draws the type-C2 affine alcove picture for the Siegel SP4/Iwahori case, where the admissible set has thirteen elements.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker explicitly draws the SP4 affine-Weyl admissible set in the Siegel/Iwahori case and counts its 13 elements
